coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on phosphate anion-quenched oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Hongwei
Wang, Hangyu
Li, Xin
Peng, Yinxian
Pan, Jianming
Niu, Xiangheng
coredata.dc:description: As alkaline phosphatase (ALP) plays crucial roles in disease warning and dephosphorylation-related cellular regulation, it is widely recognized as an important biomarker for clinical diagnosis. In this work, we proposed a facile colorimetric assay based on phosphate anion-quenched oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions for sensitive and selective detection of ALP activity. Free Ce(Ⅳ) ions exhibited a strong oxidase-like capability (providing a 40-fold catalytic turnover number compared with CeO2) to catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into its blue product TMBox mediated by dissolved O2 at neutral pH, thus triggering a remarkable color reaction visually. When PO4 3− was added, its strong affinity to Ce(Ⅳ) ions rapidly precipitated the free Ce(Ⅳ) ions, resulting in the quenching of their enzymatic ability. Given that ALP catalyzed the hydrolysis of adenosine triphosphate to produce PO4 3−, determination of ALP activity could be achieved using the colorimetric assay with no need of complicated instrumentation and protocol. As demonstrated, our assay offered a highly sensitive readout for ALP activity in two linear scopes of 0–50 U L−1 and 50–250 U L−1, providing a detection limit down to 2.3 U L−1. Besides, it could provide specific response toward ALP against other enzymes and biological species. Furthermore, the developed assay was successfully applied to evaluate ALP activity in human plasma accurately and reliably, indicating its great promise as a powerful and convenient tool for monitoring of ALP activity in clinical practice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ALP
Colorimetric assay
Ce(Ⅳ) ions
Oxidase mimic
Activity regulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PO4 3− quenches the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions
ALP-catalyzed ATP hydrolysis quenches the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions via generating PO4 3-
Ce(Ⅳ) ions exhibit the oxidase-mimicking catalytic activity
PO4 3− can quench the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions
ALP-catalyzed ATP hydrolysis can quench the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions via generating PO4 3-
Sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of ALP activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions. (A) compares the UV–Vis spectra of different systems, and the inset shows the corresponding photograph (a―Ce4++TMB, b―TMB, c―Ce4+). (B) compares the Ce(Ⅳ)-catalyzed TMB oxidation reaction in (a) N2-saturated and (b) air-saturated buffers.

PO4 3− can quench the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions. (A) presents the UV–Vis spectra of the Ce4++TMB system with the absence or presence of PO4 3−, and the inset is the corresponding photograph (a―Ce4++TMB, b―Ce4++TMB + PO4 3−). (B) compares the response of the Ce4++TMB system toward various anions (a―PO4 3-, b―Blank, c―Cl-, d―SO4 2-, e―Br-, f―NO3 -, g―SO3 2-, h―F-, i―SCN-, j―HCOO-).

Colorimetric detection of PO4 3−. (A) displays the UV–Vis spectra of the Ce4++TMB system toward different concentrations of PO4 3−. (B) presents the relationship between the absorbance at 652 nm and the PO4 3− level. (C) depicts the linear relationship. (D) compares the absorbance of the Ce4++TMB system toward various anions.

ALP-catalyzed ATP hydrolysis can quench the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions via generating PO4 3−. (A) presents the UV–Vis spectra of the Ce4++TMB system (Control) with the absence or presence of ATP and ALP, and the inset is the corresponding photograph (a―Control, b―Control + ATP, c―Control + ATP + ALP). (B) compares the response of the Ce4++TMB + ATP system toward various biological species (a―ALP, b―Trp, c―Met, d―Thr, e―Ala, f―Leu, g―GOx, h―ChOx, i―uricase, j―glucose).

Colorimetric detection of ALP activity. (A) displays the UV–Vis spectra of the Ce4++TMB + ATP system toward ALP with different activities. (B) presents the relationship between the absorbance at 652 nm and the ALP activity.
Comparison of the absorbance of the Ce4++TMB + ATP system toward various biological species.
Schematic illustration of the colorimetric ALP activity assay based on the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions that can be quenched by PO4 3−.
Reliability of our assay in detecting the ALP activity in human serum.

head.ce:title: Sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of alkaline phosphatase activity based on phosphate anion-quenched oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Green Chemistry and Chemical Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, 212003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As alkaline phosphatase (ALP) plays crucial roles in disease warning and dephosphorylation-related cellular regulation, it is widely recognized as an important biomarker for clinical diagnosis. In this work, we proposed a facile colorimetric assay based on phosphate anion-quenched oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions for sensitive and selective detection of ALP activity. Free Ce(Ⅳ) ions exhibited a strong oxidase-like capability (providing a 40-fold catalytic turnover number compared with CeO2) to catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into its blue product TMBox mediated by dissolved O2 at neutral pH, thus triggering a remarkable color reaction visually. When PO4 3− was added, its strong affinity to Ce(Ⅳ) ions rapidly precipitated the free Ce(Ⅳ) ions, resulting in the quenching of their enzymatic ability. Given that ALP catalyzed the hydrolysis of adenosine triphosphate to produce PO4 3−, determination of ALP activity could be achieved using the colorimetric assay with no need of complicated instrumentation and protocol. As demonstrated, our assay offered a highly sensitive readout for ALP activity in two linear scopes of 0–50 U L−1 and 50–250 U L−1, providing a detection limit down to 2.3 U L−1. Besides, it could provide specific response toward ALP against other enzymes and biological species. Furthermore, the developed assay was successfully applied to evaluate ALP activity in human plasma accurately and reliably, indicating its great promise as a powerful and convenient tool for monitoring of ALP activity in clinical practice.

ce:section.ce:para: As an essential enzyme that can catalyze the dephosphorylation of substrates including nucleic acids and proteins, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is widely distributed in various tissues and organs. It not only plays a significant role in regulation of intracellular processes [1,2] but also acts as a crucial biomarker for disease diagnosis [3–5]. Previous studies have revealed that the abnormal level of ALP in serum is closely correlated with bone injury [6], liver dysfunction [7], and prostate and breast cancers [8]. Thus, development of sensitive, selective, and reliable methods for ALP activity monitoring turns to be an urgent demand.

To this end, various methods, such as electrochemical [9–12], fluorescent [5,13–19], and colorimetric [20–29] approaches, have been explored for evaluation of ALP activity/level. Among these methods, colorimetric assays become the first choice of clinical practice due to their merits of simplicity, easy readout, low cost, fast response, and high throughput [20]. So far, the most widely used colorimetric assay of ALP activity is based on the conversion of colorless p-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP) into yellow p-nitrophenolate [30]. This strategy is simple and efficient, and such that it has been regarded as a standard method for ALP activity monitoring. However, it is still not practical enough because of its poor selectivity and limited linear range. Even worse, pNPP is very sensitive to light and is prone to hydrolysis spontaneously, both of which will cause false positive results [31]. Therefore, it still requires further effort to explore new colorimetric strategies with better practicability and reliability for ALP activity determination.

As emerging alternatives of some natural enzymes, nanomaterials with intrinsic enzyme-like characteristics (nanozymes) have been explored intensively in the past decade [32–35]. In comparison with bioenzymes, these nanozymes are cheaper and more stable, both of which endow them with extensive applications in biosensing [36–40]. Nevertheless, current nanomaterial-based enzyme mimics are meeting a number of challenges, including uncontrollable large-scale synthesis [41], relatively lower catalytic activity [33], and possible inertness toward bio-conjugation [42]. Instead of nanozymes, certain ions, such as Cu2+ [24,43–45], have also been found possessing enzyme-like catalytic properties. In comparison with natural enzymes and nanozymes, ionic enzyme mimics exhibit a series of attractive advantages: (1) the homogeneous nature of ionic mimics can facilitate the access of substrates to active sites; (2) ionic mimics avoid the complicated manipulation of structure, crystal surface, and size in nanozymes, thus making the catalytic system simpler; (3) these ionic mimics can be available directly from commercial reagents, avoiding the tedious preparation, purification, and storage processes in bioenzymes and nanozymes. These superiorities are supposed to make ionic enzyme mimic-catalyzed colorimetric assays possess favorable features of fast response, high sensitivity, convenience, and low cost. As a nanomaterial with the presence of mixed valence states of Ce3+ and Ce4+ and oxygen vacancies, ceria (CeO2) has been demonstrated to show multiple enzymatic activities [46–48]. Since the oxygen vacancies can compensate the reduction of positive charge by Ce3+ and thus stabilize the chemically active Ce3+ oxidation state, the Ce4+/Ce3+ redox couple plays key roles in various catalytic activities. Similar to CeO2, individual Ce4+ or Ce3+ may also possess some potential enzyme-mimicking properties.

Inspired by the above considerations, here we proposed a new strategy based on phosphate anion-quenched oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions for colorimetric determination of ALP activity. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , free Ce(Ⅳ) ions exhibit a powerful oxidase-like activity to catalyze 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into its oxide TMBox in the presence of dissolved O2 under neutral conditions, triggering a remarkable color reaction that can be distinguished by naked eyes. Further, these free ionic mimics can be precipitated by PO4 3− that originates from the hydrolysis of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) catalyzed by ALP, resulting in the quenching of their enzymatic ability. Thus, ALP activity is able to be detected based on this principle with no complicated instrumentation and protocol. As demonstrated by the followed investigations, the developed colorimetric assay could provide sensitive, selective, and accurate response toward ALP in biological matrices, suggesting its great promise as a facile and reliable tool for ALP activity/level monitoring in clinical practice.

Ce(SO4)2, ALP (30 U mg−1, from Escherichia coli), Tryptophan (Trp), Methionine (Met), Threonine (Thr), Alanine (Ala), Leucine (Leu), and cholesterol oxidase (ChOx, 10 U mg−1) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. TMB was provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Glucose oxidase (GOx, 100 U mg−1, from Aspergillus niger) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Uricase (10 U mg−1) was purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co., Ltd. Tris-HCl, acetic acid (HAc), and Na3PO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water was utilized in the whole study.

To examine the oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions, 100 μL TMB stock solution (5 mM, prepared with ethanol) and 2.76 μM Ce(Ⅳ) ions were added into 2.8 mL deionized water. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 5 min, and its UV–Vis spectrum was measured using a Cary 8454 spectrometer (Agilent Technologies Co., Ltd.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PO4 3− quenches the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions
ce:section.ce:para: 2.76 μM Ce(Ⅳ) ions were first added into 2.7 mL deionized water, and then 100 μL Na3PO4 stock solution with a certain concentration was added and mixed well. Afterwards, 100 μL TMB stock solution (5 mM, prepared with ethanol) was added into the above solution and incubated at room temperature for 5 min. Finally, UV–Vis spectra were measured using the same spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP-catalyzed ATP hydrolysis quenches the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions via generating PO4 3-
ce:section.ce:para: ATP and ALP stock solutions were prepared with 0.1 M Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.8). 100 μL ATP stock solution (10 mM) and 100 μL ALP stock solution with a certain concentration were mixed and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. After adjusting the buffer pH to neutral with diluted HAc, 100 μL Ce(IV) ions (2.76 μM) and 2.6 mL deionized water were added into the above solution. After mixed well, 100 μL TMB stock solution (5 mM, prepared with ethanol) was further added into the system and incubated at room temperature for 5 min. Finally, UV–Vis spectra were measured using the same spectrometer.

For practical detection, human serum samples provided by the Affiliated Hospital of Jiangsu University were measured using our developed assay. These serum samples were 10-fold diluted with deionized water before detection. In detail, 100 μL ATP with a concentration of 10 mM was first added into the diluted serum sample and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min; then, Ce(IV) ions were added into the mixture for reaction with TMB for 5 min; finally, UV–Vis spectra were measured using the same spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ce(Ⅳ) ions exhibit the oxidase-mimicking catalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: Since Yan's group pioneeringly reported the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Fe3O4 NPs [32], an increasing number of peroxidase-like nanozymes have been developed in the past decade. Compared with peroxidase mimics, oxidase-mimicking nanozymes seem to be more attractive in some ways, because they can catalyze reactions with no requirement of H2O2. With abundant oxygen vacancies and mixed valence states, nanoceria can exhibit multienzyme-mimicking activities, including superoxide dismutase-, peroxidase-, and oxidase-like catalytic activities [49–51]. In the present work, we employed Ce(Ⅳ) ions as a potential oxidase mimic, because its homogeneous nature may endow the ionic enzyme mimic with better activity and faster response to catalyze reactions in comparison with bioenzymes and nanozymes.

To verify the oxidase-like catalytic activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions, TMB was used as a typical oxidase chromogenic substrate. As presented in Fig. 1 A, individual TMB or Ce4+ exhibits no UV–Vis absorption, suggesting their negligible color background. It is interestingly found that a color reaction from colorless to blue occurs when the two species are mixed together, providing a remarkable UV–Vis absorption peak at around 652 nm. In addition to the positively charged TMB substrate, another two chromogenic substrates, the negatively charged 2,2′-azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiozoline)-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) and the neutral o-phenylenediamine (OPD), were also used to check the oxidase-mimicking ability of Ce(Ⅳ) ions. As observed from Fig. S1 (Supporting Information), all substrates undergo a remarkable color reaction under the catalysis of Ce(Ⅳ) ions. These results confirm the oxidase-like ability of Ce(Ⅳ) ions. Even so, there is still a concern about whether the Ce(Ⅳ) ions used play an oxidase mimic role or just act as an oxidant [46,52]. To uncover the underlying catalytic mechanism of Ce(Ⅳ) ions, we investigated the O2-dependent catalytic activity of the ionic enzyme mimic to further confirm its oxidase-mimicking property. As demonstrated in Fig. 1B, it is found that the colorless TMB substrate can be oxidized into its blue product TMBox prominently in the air-saturated buffer, while in the N2-saturated buffer the color reaction is significantly suppressed. This contrast suggests that O2 is required for the catalytic reaction, also validating that Ce(Ⅳ) ions indeed act as an oxidase mimic rather than an oxidant in our study. Besides, we also checked whether Ce(Ⅳ) ions can play other enzymatic activities such as peroxidase. As shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information), Ce(Ⅳ) ions also exhibit a weak peroxidase-mimicking activity, while its peroxidase-like activity seems to be much lower than its oxidase activity. Previous studies [46,49] have uncovered the molecular mechanism for the oxidase-mimicking activity of nanoceria: the absorbed O2 at the defect sites of nanoceria is first reduced to superoxide radicals by Ce3+, and then the generated superoxide radicals trigger the oxidation of TMB into its product TMBox. It is reasonable to suppose that the effortless transformation between Ce(Ⅳ) and Ce(Ⅲ) mutually [53], similar to nanoceria, results in the following oxidase-like catalytic processes of Ce(Ⅳ) ions:
(1)
Ce
4 +
↔
Ce
3 +
(2)
Ce
3 +
+
O
2
→
Ce
4 +
+
O
2
⋅ −
(3)
O
2
⋅ −
+
TMB
→
H
2
O
+
TMBox

What should be stated is that when Ce(Ⅲ) ions with the same concentration as Ce(Ⅳ) ions are used as an oxidase mimic, the obtained catalytic effect is not as good as that observed in Ce(Ⅳ) ions (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). This does not surprise us, because the enzyme-mimicking properties of Ce-based materials are closely related to the Ce4+/Ce3+ ratio rather than individual Ce3+ or Ce4+ [33,54]. In this regard, Ce(Ⅳ) ions are used for further study.

As expected, the TMB color reaction highly depends on the amount of Ce(Ⅳ) ions used. With the increasing concentrations of the ionic enzyme mimic, the color reaction turns to be faster and more remarkable (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). Additionally, the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions is pH-dependent. As presented in Figure S5 (Supporting Information), with the increase of the buffer pH from weak acidic to neutral, the absorbance obtained from the same system increases correspondingly, indicating that Ce(Ⅳ) ions can exhibit a higher catalytic activity in the neutral solution. When the buffer pH further increases to alkaline, it is found that the reaction solution almost remains colorless, and the absorbance at 652 nm is negligible. This suggests that no TMBox is produced, which in turn indicates that the catalytic reaction does not proceed. This phenomenon may be related to the instability of these free Ce(Ⅳ) ions in alkaline conditions, in which Ce(Ⅳ) ions can be precipitated by hydroxyl ions.

To further understand the oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions, the steady-state kinetic study on the TMB color reaction was carried out at room temperature. As displayed in Fig. S6 (Supporting Information), a typical Michaelis-Menten curve is fitted for the Ce(Ⅳ) mimic toward TMB. Values for the Michaelis-Menten constant (K m), maximum velocity (V max), and turnover number (k cat) are obtained from this plot and compared with those of other oxidase mimics (Table S1, Supporting Information). Obviously, the apparent K
m value of Ce(Ⅳ) ions toward TMB is much lower than that of Ce-based oxidase-like nanozymes, indicating that the free Ce(Ⅳ) ions have a better affinity toward the substrate. Although the V max value of Ce(Ⅳ) ions is slightly lower than that of the oxidase-like nanozymes including CeVO4 [55] and CeO2 [56], the catalytic turnover number (k cat) is determined to be 6.43 s−1, 45-fold and 40-fold higher of CeVO4 and CeO2, respectively. This exciting activity is closely related to the homogeneous nature of the Ce(Ⅳ) ions used. Compared with nanozymes that are dispersed in buffer as solids, the free Ce(Ⅳ) ions can greatly facilitate the access of substrates to active sites, thus promoting the catalytic rate. In view of the excellent oxidase-like activity, they can be employed as a promising substitute of natural oxidase for various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PO4 3− can quench the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidase-mimicking activity of the Ce(Ⅳ) ions used can be quenched by PO4 3−. As seen from Fig. 2 A, the mixture of Ce4+ and TMB leads to a remarkable UV–Vis absorption peak at approximately 652 nm. When PO4 3− is added into the system, it is found that the TMB color reaction catalyzed by Ce(Ⅳ) ions is significantly suppressed. Correspondingly, the absorption signal turns to be very weak. This result indicates that PO4 3− is able to quench the oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions. This quenching originates from the precipitation of the active Ce4+ by PO4 3−. The phosphate anion has very strong affinities toward both Ce4+ and Ce3+, and it can rapidly precipitate the free Ce4+ and Ce3+ ions, thus resulting in the quenching of the Ce4+ oxidase-like activity (Scheme 1). Furthermore, the activity quenching of Ce4+ ions shows a good specificity toward the phosphate anion against other common anions. Only PO4 3− can quench the oxidase-like activity of Ce4+ ions, while other ions exhibit negligible effects on the TMB color reaction catalyzed by Ce4+ (Fig. 2B). We further checked whether adenosine and other phosphate-containing species, including adenosine monophosphate (AMP), adenosine diphosphate (ADP), and adenosine triphosphate (ATP), could quench the TMB color reaction catalyzed by Ce4+. As shown in Fig. S7 (Supporting Information), it is found that only AMP as well as PO4 3− can quench the color reaction obviously, while other species cannot. This indicates that Ce4+ ions might be precipitated only by monophosphate.

The above finding enables us to detect the PO4 3− species selectively using the colorimetric strategy. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 A, the absorbance of the Ce4++TMB color reaction is highly dependent on the amount of PO4 3− added. As expected, the UV–Vis absorption peak decreases gradually with the increasing concentrations of PO4 3−. The relationship between the absorbance at 652 nm and the PO4 3− level is presented in Fig. 3B. It is further found that a linear relationship exists when the PO4 3− concentration ranges from 0.83 μM to 8.33 μM (Fig. 3C). As Ce4+ can be precipitated by PO4 3− specifically against other anions, the colorimetric strategy exhibits a good selectivity for PO4 3− sensing. As confirmed by Fig. 3D, only PO4 3− results in the significant decrease of the absorbance at 652 nm, while other common anions make no obvious response different from the blank group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP-catalyzed ATP hydrolysis can quench the oxidase-like activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions via generating PO4 3-
ce:section.ce:para: Given that ALP can catalyze the hydrolysis of phosphoryl esters in a weak medium to produce PO4 3−, the presence of both ALP and a suitable substrate can also trigger the suppression of the TMB color reaction catalyzed by Ce(Ⅳ) ions. In this work, ATP was selected as the ALP substrate due to its stability in such a condition. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, addition of only ATP can still not inhibit the color reaction. However, when both ATP and ALP are added into the Ce4++TMB (Control) system, the color reaction is significantly suppressed. This phenomenon is also confirmed by the time-dependent absorbance changes of corresponding systems (Fig. S8, Supporting Information). The result originates from the catalysis of ALP toward ATP and the phosphate anion-quenched oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions (Scheme 1): ALP first catalyzes the dephosphorylation of ATP to produce PO4 3−, and the generated PO4 3− species then precipitates the free Ce(Ⅳ) ions to quench their enzymatic ability. It is further found that the Ce4++TMB + ATP reaction system is inhibited only by ALP. As verified by Fig. 4B, amino acids (Trp, Met, Thr, Ala, and Leu), enzymes (GOx, ChOx, and uricase), and glucose that usually coexist in biological matrices cannot trigger any change of the reaction system. This reveals the excellent specificity of the principle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of ALP activity
ce:section.ce:para: As the Ce4++TMB + ATP reaction can be inhibited by ALP, it can be used as a facile colorimetric assay for ALP activity monitoring. In clinical diagnosis and biomedical study, determination of ALP activity is of great importance, because it not only plays a significant role in regulation of intracellular processes [2] but also acts as a crucial biomarker for various diseases [4]. Fig. 5 A displays the UV–Vis spectra of the Ce4++TMB + ATP system toward ALP with different activities. Obviously, the absorption signals decrease along with the increasing activities of ALP. When a small amount of ALP is added, a fast decrease of the absorbance at 652 nm is observed. This means that a small number of PO4 3− generated from ATP hydrolysis by ALP can be rapidly precipitated by the sufficient Ce4+ ions. When more ALP is added, a relatively slow decrease of the absorbance is obtained. This is due to that the overmuch PO4 3− cannot be precipitated by the limited Ce4+ ions timely. As a result, two linear ranges of 0–50 U L−1 and 50–250 U L−1 are found between the absorbance at 652 nm and the ALP activity (Fig. 5B). According to the signal-to-noise of three (S/N = 3) rule, a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 2.3 U L−1 is determined. The sensitivity, linear range, and LOD can fully satisfy the demand for ALP activity detection in clinical practice. In comparison with previously reported methods for ALP activity determination (Table S2, Supporting Information), the analytical performance of our assay is comparable or even better. More attractively, our assay exhibits the followed two merits over other methods: (1) compared with fluorescent sensors, our colorimetric assay can offer comparable performance with simpler equipment. Smartphone or even naked eyes can be used for signal readout and evaluation [57]; (2) the Ce(Ⅳ) ions used as a sensing element in our assay can be available directly from commercial reagents, and no synthetic process is required [5,14,15,17,19]. Specially, in comparison with the signal-on method proposed by Park and co-workers [43], in which the assay relied on the pyrophosphate (PPi) inhibition of the peroxidase activity of copper ions, although our LOD is inferior, our proposed method can successfully avoid the potential interference from pyrophosphatase (PPase) that exists in clinical samples. Thus, our assay will have better selectivity for ALP activity monitoring in clinical practice. These advantages endow it with great potential for applications.

In addition to high sensitivity and low LOD, the developed assay is also highly selective for ALP activity determination. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 , common substances coexisting in human plasma, including amino acids, other enzymes, and small biological molecules, will not make obvious effects on the response of our assay. This result indicates that the developed assay can be used for the selective determination of ALP activity.

Having demonstrated the feasibility of our colorimetric assay, we further investigated the reliability of our developed assay for evaluation of ALP activity in clinical samples. As the normal level of ALP activity in adults is in the range of 46–190 U L−1, our assay is sensitive enough for practical detection of ALP activity in human plasma. In this regard, four serum specimens provided by the Affiliated Hospital of Jiangsu University (Zhenjiang, China) were tested using the develop method. Further, the detection results obtained by our assay were compared with the clinical results provided by the hospital. As listed in Table 1 , the results for all samples tested by our assay are in good agreement with the clinical data, providing the relative errors in the range of ±10%. Thus, the colorimetric assay we developed shows great potential for clinical applications.

In summary, we have developed a facile colorimetric assay for sensitive and selective detection of ALP activity on the basis of phosphate anion-quenched oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions. Our colorimetric strategy not only offers a sensitive and selective readout for ALP activity but also needs no complicated instrumentation and protocol. More attractively, all sensing elements can be available directly from commercial reagents. This significantly simplifies related operations and detection cost, making it more promising in practical applications. As demonstrated, the developed assay with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity can be applied to monitor the ALP activity in human serum accurately and reliably, suggesting its good practicability and reliability for ALP activity determination in clinical practice. This contribution, on the one hand, uncovers the “hidden” high oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce(Ⅳ) ions, and on the other hand, provides a new approach for convenient monitoring of ALP activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21605061 and 31601549), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20160489), the Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province (No. 16KJB150009), the Postdoctoral Fund of China (No. 2016M600365), the Postdoctoral Fund of Jiangsu Province (No. 1601015B), the Open Fund from the Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry (No. SKLFMC201601), the Open Fund from the State Key Laboratory of Bioreactor University, and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX18_2341).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Supporting Information Supporting Information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.09.045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural basis for control by phosphorylation

-/- mouse model of infantile hypophosphatasia
